primary_hypothesis: A Shift-B shared assay input, specifically a reagent-lot effect, increased abnormal calls across both samples and sealed controls.
rival_hypothesis: Incomplete or ineffective pre-shift cleaning contaminated sample-contact surfaces and thereby increased Shift-B sample abnormalities.
predicted_discrimination: A substantial sealed-control gap similar to the 9-percentage-point sample gap would support a Shift-B factor that also affects controls, such as the candidate shared reagent-lot effect, and would weigh against sample-surface contamination as the sole explanation. Little or no sealed-control gap would weigh against the candidate and leave the cleaning-related rival more plausible for the sample-only anomaly.
bounded_conclusion: Within the supplied Shift-A and Shift-B records, the parallel increase from 1/100 to 10/100 in samples and in sealed controls supports a Shift-B-wide assay factor over sample-surface cleaning failure as the sole cause; the records do not identify the reagent lot as the confirmed factor.
residual_uncertainty: The shared reagent-lot effect has not been directly tested, and another Shift-B-wide factor capable of affecting both sealed controls and samples could produce the same pattern.
recommended_consequence: Quarantine the affected Shift-B assay results and escalate for a reagent-lot comparison and review of other shared Shift-B assay inputs; do not assign disciplinary blame on these records.
